medications.indication_rationale: An innocent murmur requires no medication or treatment; no paediatric prescribing is appropriate.
contraindications_in_this_case: No medication warranted for a benign innocent murmur

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Restricting the child's sport/activity or keeping him awake to watch him overnight
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Activity restriction and vigilance are unwarranted and harmful for a well child with an innocent murmur, reinforcing parental anxiety; normal activity should be encouraged.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Let the child continue all normal activities and competitive sport without restriction.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Full participation is safe and demonstrates a healthy heart; restriction is unwarranted and harmful.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Encourage the parents to treat the child normally (allow normal sleep and play) and avoid hypervigilant checking.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Normalising routine breaks the parental anxiety cycle and supports the child's wellbeing.

patient_education_points.point: A murmur is very often just the sound of normal blood flowing through a healthy heart - extremely common in healthy children and usually fades as they grow. In a child who is thriving, plays sport with no trouble, and has no worrying symptoms, this is very likely a harmless (innocent) murmur.

He does not need an emergency hospital visit tonight, and he can sleep and play completely normally. Please book a routine GP appointment in the next week or two to listen to his heart and confirm it, which is all that's usually needed.
There's no need to restrict his soccer or activities - continuing to play is safe and is itself a good sign of a healthy heart.

Seek medical care if he ever faints, gets chest pain or breathlessness on exertion, turns blue around the lips, or becomes unusually lethargic - those would be the signs that need checking, but you have none of them now.

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine in-person GP review within 1-2 weeks for auscultation and reassurance
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Confirmation of the innocent murmur's benign features on auscultation, ongoing absence of symptoms, and documentation; referral/echocardiography only if concerning features emerge
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any syncope, exertional chest pain or breathlessness, cyanosis, or unusual lethargy - seek prompt medical assessment.

must_include_items: Appropriate, warm reassurance that this is very likely an innocent murmur in a well, thriving child
Avoidance of unnecessary emergency/overnight paediatric ED presentation and over-investigation
No activity restriction - encourage normal sport/play
Routine in-person GP confirmation within 1-2 weeks
Retained safety-netting for genuine red flags (syncope, exertional symptoms, cyanosis, lethargy)
acceptable_alternatives: Routine GP review within 1-2 weeks or a non-urgent paediatric review are acceptable; echocardiography only if the GP finds concerning features
'Harmless/innocent murmur', 'normal blood-flow sound' are acceptable framings
errors_of_omission: Failing to reassure, leaving the parents frightened
Not addressing the specific parental fear (sudden death in sleep)
errors_of_commission: Sending a well, asymptomatic child to the emergency department overnight (over-triage)
Ordering an urgent echocardiogram/cardiology work-up unnecessarily
Restricting the child's sport/activity without cause
Being dismissive in a way that fails to calm anxiety or, conversely, catastrophizing
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: OVER-TRIAGE-AVOIDANCE (TRUE-NEGATIVE) CASE: A pass requires (1) appropriate reassurance and routine confirmation without emergency over-triage or over-investigation, (2) no activity restriction, AND (3) retained red-flag safety-netting. Sending this well child to the ED overnight, or restricting sport, is an over-triage failure; omitting red-flag safety-netting is an under-triage failure.
